questions.rationale: Clause 3962(5)(iii) requires policies and procedures that specifically address the handling of futures contracts, forward contracts and similar derivatives with pending delivery months — without a documented process, an uninstructed long position can slide into a physical delivery obligation, the exposure clause 3963(1)(xi) requires the firm to detect. Option D is the closest distractor because client notification under 3962(4) includes approaching expiry dates, but expiry notification is a 3962(4) item and the question asks for the 3962(5) pending-delivery-month handling requirement; B and C concern CFD position size and solicitation approval, neither in the facts.
questions.source_quote: "address the handling of futures contracts, forward contracts and similar derivatives with pending delivery months," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8327

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc. is an Investment Dealer with eight retail branches. Supervisor Lise Arsenault conducts the first-tier reviews for the Rivière-Claire branch. This week her daily and monthly review queues surface the following from the book of Registered Representative Tomas Beck: (1) in one retiree's account, four trades were cancelled and rebooked into a different account number over two weeks, and the client has not responded to calls; (2) Beck personally bought 5,000 shares of a thinly traded issuer in his employee account minutes before entering a large buy order for a client in the same issuer; (3) Lise realizes the branch's review population, generated from client account codes, has never included Beck's own employee account, even though it trades actively; and (4) Lise keeps all of her review records and follow-up notes in paper files at the Rivière-Claire branch, which head office has never visited to inspect.
questions.stem: Item (1) shows repeated trade cancellations and rebookings into a different account number in the retiree's account. Under subsection 3945(2), which detection category do these facts most directly indicate?

options.A: Deterioration of the quality of client holdings in an account
options.B: Outstanding margin calls
options.C: Undisclosed short sales
options.D: Excessive transaction transfers and trade cancellations indicating possible unauthorized trading

questions.rationale: Clause 3945(2)(vi) requires retail supervision policies to address detecting excessive transaction transfers and trade cancellations indicating possible unauthorized trading — cancelling and rebooking trades across account numbers is how unauthorized activity gets concealed, and the unreachable client strengthens the inference. Account number changes are themselves a separate flag under 3945(2)(xii), reinforcing the same concern. Option A describes declining portfolio quality, not booking manipulation; B and C are unrelated margin and settlement flags with no support in the facts.
questions.source_quote: "excessive transaction transfers and trade cancellations indicating possible unauthorized trading," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8153

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc. is an Investment Dealer with eight retail branches. Supervisor Lise Arsenault conducts the first-tier reviews for the Rivière-Claire branch. This week her daily and monthly review queues surface the following from the book of Registered Representative Tomas Beck: (1) in one retiree's account, four trades were cancelled and rebooked into a different account number over two weeks, and the client has not responded to calls; (2) Beck personally bought 5,000 shares of a thinly traded issuer in his employee account minutes before entering a large buy order for a client in the same issuer; (3) Lise realizes the branch's review population, generated from client account codes, has never included Beck's own employee account, even though it trades actively; and (4) Lise keeps all of her review records and follow-up notes in paper files at the Rivière-Claire branch, which head office has never visited to inspect.
questions.stem: Item (2) — Beck buying shares in his employee account minutes before entering the client's large buy order in the same thinly traded issuer — engages which detection item that subsection 3945(2) requires retail supervision policies to address?

options.A: Late payment
options.B: Undue concentration of securities in a single account
options.C: Front running
options.D: Inappropriate or high risk trading strategies

questions.rationale: Clause 3945(2)(xi) lists front running among the matters retail supervision policies must detect — trading the employee account ahead of a known client order that can move a thin stock is the textbook pattern, and it simultaneously raises the improper or excessive employee trading flag in 3945(2)(x) and the conflict between Registered Representative and client trading activity in 3945(2)(v). Option D is the closest distractor because the conduct is certainly inappropriate, but that clause targets the strategy traded in client accounts, not trading ahead of a client; A and B have no factual basis here.
questions.source_quote: "(x) improper or excessive employee trading, (xi) front running, (xii) account number changes," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8159

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc. is an Investment Dealer with eight retail branches. Supervisor Lise Arsenault conducts the first-tier reviews for the Rivière-Claire branch. This week her daily and monthly review queues surface the following from the book of Registered Representative Tomas Beck: (1) in one retiree's account, four trades were cancelled and rebooked into a different account number over two weeks, and the client has not responded to calls; (2) Beck personally bought 5,000 shares of a thinly traded issuer in his employee account minutes before entering a large buy order for a client in the same issuer; (3) Lise realizes the branch's review population, generated from client account codes, has never included Beck's own employee account, even though it trades actively; and (4) Lise keeps all of her review records and follow-up notes in paper files at the Rivière-Claire branch, which head office has never visited to inspect.
questions.stem: Item (3) reveals that the branch review population has never included Beck's own actively trading employee account. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance, why is this a defect in Vaillancourt's review design?

options.A: It is not a defect — employee accounts are exempt from account activity review because employees are already supervised as registrants
options.B: Dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review — employee accounts are exactly where conflicts such as trading ahead of clients surface, so excluding them blinds the review to that risk
options.C: Employee accounts need only be reviewed by CIRO during its periodic examinations
options.D: Employee accounts must be reviewed, but only if the employee consents in writing

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.1.2 states Dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review — and the guidance's example second-tier criteria likewise capture non-client trading. Excluding Beck's employee account is what allowed his trading ahead of the client order in item (2) to go unflagged at the account level. Option A is the misconception the guidance forecloses: registrant status does not exempt the account from activity review; C shifts the dealer's own supervision duty onto the regulator; D invents a consent condition — supervision of non-client account activity is not optional for the employee.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 204

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc. is an Investment Dealer with eight retail branches. Supervisor Lise Arsenault conducts the first-tier reviews for the Rivière-Claire branch. This week her daily and monthly review queues surface the following from the book of Registered Representative Tomas Beck: (1) in one retiree's account, four trades were cancelled and rebooked into a different account number over two weeks, and the client has not responded to calls; (2) Beck personally bought 5,000 shares of a thinly traded issuer in his employee account minutes before entering a large buy order for a client in the same issuer; (3) Lise realizes the branch's review population, generated from client account codes, has never included Beck's own employee account, even though it trades actively; and (4) Lise keeps all of her review records and follow-up notes in paper files at the Rivière-Claire branch, which head office has never visited to inspect.
questions.stem: Item (4): Lise's review records and follow-up notes are kept in paper files at the Rivière-Claire branch, and head office has never inspected them. Under subsection 3908(4), what does keeping supervision records at a branch office oblige Vaillancourt to do?

options.A: Conduct periodic on-site reviews of branch office supervision and record keeping
options.B: Nothing — the location of supervision records is left entirely to the branch's discretion
options.C: Digitize and transmit every record to CIRO within 30 days of creation
options.D: Move all supervision records to head office, since branch storage is prohibited

questions.rationale: Subsection 3908(4) provides that where supervision records are kept at a branch office, the Dealer Member must conduct periodic on-site reviews of branch office supervision and record keeping — branch storage is permitted, but it triggers head-office verification that the records exist and the supervision is real. Option D is the closest distractor because it correctly senses a control is needed but imposes the wrong one — the rule permits branch-kept records and responds with on-site review, not relocation; B ignores the trigger entirely; C invents CIRO transmission, when the obligation under 3908(5) is retention for the section 3803 period, available for inspection.
questions.source_quote: "Where supervision records are kept at a branch office, the Dealer Member must conduct periodic on-site reviews of branch office supervision and record keeping." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7971
